blurb: Tender chicken in a creamy, mildly spiced tomato butter sauce.

prov.famous_for: Creamy tomato-based curry with mild spices
prov.consists_of: Marinated chicken in tomato-butter cream sauce
prov.name_origin: Murgh Makhani, created at Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi, 1950s by Kundan Lal Gujral
prov.why_loved: Approachable heat level, rich velvety sauce, universal appeal
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — marinated chicken broiled then simmered in sauce

brief.lead: Butter chicken (murgh makhani) is Delhi's gift to the world—a mild, creamy tomato curry that makes first-time curry eaters feel welcome. Chicken thighs marinate briefly in yogurt and spices, get broiled for char, then simmer gently in a velvety sauce of tomatoes, butter, cream, and fenugreek. It's the Friday night takeout favourite that actually works at home.
expect: The sauce is luxurious but balanced by bright tomato and gentle spices.
Chili powder provides gentle heat—not a fiery dish.
Boneless thigh meat stays juicy through broiling and simmering.
Kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves) is the signature finish.

ing: Greek yogurt or full-fat plain yogurt
chili powder or Kashmiri chili powder
tl: Mix chicken with yogurt, spices, ginger-garlic paste, salt
Set broiler to high or oven to 220°C fan, line tray with foil
Broil marinated chicken 12-15 min until charred in spots, flip halfway
Sauté aromatics in butter-oil, add spices, tomatoes, paste
Add cream, kasuri methi, simmer 10 min until thickened
Add charred chicken to sauce, heat through, garnish
steps: Marinate the chicken

Cut boneless chicken thighs into bite-sized pieces. In a bowl, combine Greek yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, chili powder, cumin, garam masala, and salt. Add chicken, mix well to coat, and marinate at room temperature for at least 20 minutes or up to 1 hour. Don't exceed 24 hours or the texture degrades.
WHY: Yogurt tenderises and adds tang; spices penetrate; brief marination balances flavour and texture.
Broil for char

Preheat your broiler to high (or oven to 220°C fan). Line a baking tray with foil. Arrange marinated chicken in a single layer. Broil for 12-15 minutes, flipping halfway, until chicken is cooked through and has dark charred spots in places. The charring is essential—it's where the smoky depth comes from.
WHY: High-heat broiling mimics the tandoor, creating the char that defines restaurant butter chicken.
Build the sauce base

In a heavy-based saucepan or Dutch oven, heat butter and oil over medium heat. Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 1 minute until fragrant. Add cumin, chili powder, garam masala, and salt; cook 30 seconds. Add crushed tomatoes and tomato paste, stir well, and bring to a simmer.
WHY: Cooking spices in fat blooms their flavours; tomato paste adds depth and richness.
Simmer for depth

Reduce heat to low and simmer the tomato base for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it thickens and deepens in colour. The oil may start to separate slightly—that's fine. Stir in double cream and crushed kasuri methi. Simmer another 5 minutes until the sauce is velvety and coats the back of a spoon.
WHY: Simmering concentrates the tomato flavour and develops sweetness; kasuri methi is the signature aromatic.
Combine and serve

Add the broiled chicken to the sauce, stirring gently to coat. Simmer for 2-3 minutes just until heated through—don't overcook or the chicken will dry out. Taste and adjust salt if needed. Serve hot, garnished with fresh coriander, alongside naan or basmati rice.
WHY: Gentle heating integrates the flavours without toughening the chicken; finishing with coriander adds freshness.
pair: Off-dry whites cut through richness without fighting mild spices
Cool, creamy, sweet-tart refreshment that echoes the dish's flavours

blurb: Rice noodles stir-fried with tamarind, fish sauce, egg, and crunchy toppings.

prov.famous_for: Perfect balance of sweet, sour, salty flavours
prov.consists_of: Rice noodles with tamarind-fish sauce-palm sugar, egg, tofu, prawns, peanuts
prov.name_origin: Pad Thai, national dish of Thailand, mid-20th century
prov.why_loved: Addictive sweet-sour-salty balance, varied textures, quick cooking
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — rehydrated noodles stir-fried in small batches over high heat

brief.lead: Pad Thai is Thailand's great ambassador—the noodle dish that conquered the world by getting everything right: tight rice noodles wok-charred in a sauce of tamarind (sour), fish sauce (salty), and palm sugar (sweet), with egg for richness, prawns for sweet-savour, tofu for body, and a crown of peanuts, bean sprouts, and garlic chives for crunch. The secret? Cook in small batches and use tamarind, not ketchup.
expect: Medium rice noodles, tender with bounce, not mushy.
Soft egg, crisp sprouts, crunchy peanuts.
Lime, chilli powder, more peanuts at the table.

ing: Chinese flat garlic chives, cut into pieces
tl: Cover rice noodles with warm (110°F) water, soak 30 min until pliable
Mix sliced pork with fish sauce and sugar, set aside
Heat palm sugar and tamarind until sugar dissolves, stir in fish sauce
High-heat wok: sear pork, add aromatics, egg, scramble briefly
Add noodles, sauce, water, stir until sauce absorbs (2 min)
Add half chives and sprouts, wilt 30 sec, turn off heat, fold in remaining toppings
steps: Rehydrate the noodles
Place dried medium rice noodles in a bowl and cover with slightly warm water (about 110°F/43°C). Let soak for 30 minutes until pliable. Drain well. Do not pre-boil—they'll cook in the sauce.
WHY: Soaking rehydrates without making them mushy; pre-boiling leads to clumping.
Marinate the pork
While noodles soak, slice pork shoulder very thinly (partially freeze if helpful). Toss with fish sauce and a pinch of sugar. Set aside.
WHY: Brief marination seasons the pork and adds a touch of sweetness.
Mix the sauce

In a small saucepan over low heat, combine palm sugar and tamarind concentrate. Stir until sugar dissolves (add 1 tbsp water if needed). Remove from heat and stir in fish sauce until well combined. Set aside.
WHY: The sauce must be ready before the wok heats up—Pad Thai cooks fast.
Stir-fry the base

Heat a wok or heavy skillet over high heat until lightly smoking. Add oil and swirl. Sear pork 20 seconds until halfway cooked. Add tofu, sweet preserved radish, dried shrimp, and shallot; stir-fry 40 seconds until aromatic. Push to one side. Crack egg into empty side, scramble briefly until mostly set, then fold everything together, breaking egg into small pieces.
WHY: Cooking egg before noodles prevents it from becoming scrambled bits; early aromatics build depth.
Cook noodles with sauce

Add rehydrated noodles to wok. Pour sauce around edges, along with 1 tbsp plus 2 tsp water. Stir-fry constantly for 2 minutes until sauce is fully absorbed and noodles are tender-cooked. Add half the garlic chives and half the bean sprouts; toss 30 seconds until just wilted.
WHY: Sauce finishes cooking the noodles; half the chives/sprouts cook down, the rest stay fresh.
Finish and serve

Turn off the heat. Add remaining garlic chives, bean sprouts, and crushed peanuts. Toss gently to combine. Serve immediately with extra crushed peanuts, sugar, lime wedges, Thai chilli powder, and fish sauce on the side for diners to adjust.
WHY: Adding half the fresh toppings off heat preserves their crunch; customisation is authentic to Thai style.
pair: Aromatic whites with slight sweetness balance the sharp-sour-salty flavours
Cool, creamy, sweet—mirrors the dish's flavour profile

blurb: Marinated pork with pineapple, cooked on a vertical spit, adapted for home.

prov.famous_for: Pork marinated with chiles and pineapple, carved from vertical spit
prov.consists_of: Thinly sliced marinated pork, pineapple chunks, onions, cilantro on corn tortillas
prov.name_origin: Tacos al pastor ('shepherd style'), from Puebla, Mexico, early 1900s
prov.why_loved: Complex chile flavour, sweet-tart pineapple contrast, addictive eating
prov.method_detail: Stovetop/grill — marinated pork seared and served with fresh pineapple

brief.lead: Tacos al pastor is Mexico's Lebanese-inspired masterpiece—pork shoulder, thinly sliced and marinated in dried chiles, achiote, vinegar, and pineapple, then traditionally stacked on a vertical spit (trompo) and roasted until the edges crisp and char. At home, we adapt by marinating thinly cut pork, searing it hot, and serving with fresh pineapple, onions, and cilantro on warm corn tortillas with salsa. The pineapple isn't just garnish—it's in the marinade and on the taco, sweet-tart against smoky pork.
expect: Chile-marinated pork with charred edges, tender within.
Fresh pineapple balances the rich pork—some tacos include grilled pineapple.
Two-bite corn tortillas, easy to eat multiples.
Serve with salsa verde, roja, or both.

ing: pork shoulder or butt, thinly sliced
dried guajillo chiles, stemmed and seeded
dried ancho or pasilla chiles, stemmed and seeded
apple cider vinegar or white vinegar
tl: Boil dried chiles in water 15 min until soft, drain
Blend softened chiles with vinegar, pineapple juice, garlic, onion, spices
Coat sliced pork in marinade, refrigerate 30 min (or overnight)
Heat corn tortillas on griddle or dry skillet, keep warm
Sear marinated pork over high heat 2-3 min per side until charred and cooked
Fill tortillas with pork, pineapple, onion, cilantro, serve with salsa, lime
steps: Prepare the chiles
Bring a small saucepan of water to boil. Remove from heat, add dried guajillo and ancho chiles, and soak for 15 minutes until softened. Drain, reserving a splash of soaking liquid.
WHY: Rehydrating dried chiles softens them for blending; the soaking liquid adds depth.
Make the marinade

In a blender, combine the softened chiles, vinegar, pineapple juice, achiote paste (or powder), garlic, half the onion (roughly chopped), cumin, oregano, salt, and pepper. Blend until completely smooth, adding a tablespoon of the chile soaking liquid if needed to get it moving.
WHY: Blending creates a smooth, intensely flavoured paste that penetrates the pork.
Marinate the pork

Slice pork shoulder against the grain into very thin strips (partially freeze for easier slicing). Place in a bowl or dish, pour the marinade over, and toss to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes, up to overnight.
WHY: Thin slices absorb marinade quickly; the vinegar tenderises and chiles add colour and flavour.
Cook the pork

Heat a large heavy skillet or grill over high heat until very hot. Shake excess marinade from pork (discard marinade) and add pork in batches—don't crowd the pan. Sear for 2-3 minutes per side until charred in spots and cooked through. Transfer to a board and rest for 5 minutes, then chop into bite-sized pieces.
WHY: High heat creates char; resting prevents juices from running out.
Warm the tortillas
While pork cooks, heat corn tortillas on a dry skillet or griddle over medium-high heat for about 30 seconds per side until pliable and slightly charred. Stack and wrap in a cloth to keep warm.
WHY: Warm tortillas fold without cracking; charring adds flavour.
Assemble and serve

Dice the remaining onion and cut pineapple into small chunks. For each taco, fill a warm tortilla with chopped pork, pineapple, onion, and cilantro. Serve with lime wedges and salsa on the side. Diners can add salsa and squeeze lime to taste.
WHY: The classic trio—pork, pineapple, onion—creates the perfect bite; fresh garnishes brighten the rich pork.
pair: Beer cuts fat and spice, refreshes palate between bites
Bitter-sweet, tart, refreshing—matches pineapple and chiles

blurb: Rich, creamy pork bone broth served with noodles, chashu pork, and toppings.

prov.famous_for: Opaque, rich, creamy pork bone broth
prov.consists_of: Pork and chicken bone broth simmered 12+ hours, served with noodles, chashu, egg
prov.name_origin: Tonkotsu ('pork bone'), from Fukuoka, Kyushu, Japan, mid-20th century
prov.why_loved: Incredibly rich, umami-packed broth with velvety texture
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — long rolling boil emulsifies collagen into creamy broth

brief.lead: Tonkotsu ramen is the bowl that changed how the world thinks about soup—a broth so rich, opaque, and creamy you'd swear there's cream or milk, but it's all from bones. Pork trotters, chicken carcasses, and aromatics are boiled at a rolling boil for 12-plus hours, breaking down collagen until it emulsifies fat and marrow into the liquid, creating a pale, milky soup with body like light cream. Served with springy noodles, tender chashu pork, a soft-yolk egg, and garnishes, it's the ultimate comfort bowl—worth the long cook.
expect: Pale, opaque, intensely porky—rich but balanced, not cloying.
Thin, alkaline noodles with bounce—made for slurping.
Add egg, spring onions, sesame seeds, chilli oil to taste.

ing: chashu pork belly slices (marinated and braised)
tl: Cover pork and chicken bones with cold water, bring to boil, dump immediately
Pan-sear onion, garlic, ginger until deeply charred (15 min)
Scrub all bones under cold water until grey/white, remove all dark bits
Return bones to pot with charred veg, leeks, scallions, mushrooms, fatback, cover with cold water, bring to rolling boil
Lift out pork fatback when tender, refrigerate for later
Boil until opaque and creamy (6-8 more hours), strain through cheesecloth
Whisk in chopped fatback, season with soy/salt, serve with noodles and toppings
steps: Blanch the bones

Place pork trotters and chicken carcasses in a large stockpot and cover with cold water. Bring to a boil over high heat. As soon as it boils, dump the water and bones down the drain. This initial boil tightens proteins and releases blood.
WHY: Blanching removes impurities that would cloud the broth—this is the key to a clean, pale tonkotsu.
Char the aromatics

While bones blanch, heat vegetable oil in a cast iron skillet over high heat until smoking. Add onion (skin on), garlic cloves, and ginger chunks. Cook, tossing occasionally, until deeply charred on most sides—about 15 minutes. The darker, the better. Set aside.
WHY: Charring creates Maillard reactions that add deep umami to the broth.
Clean the bones thoroughly

After blanching, wash every bone under cold running water, scrubbing with your hands or a chopstick to remove every bit of dark marrow, coagulated blood, or brown discoloration. Bones should be uniformly grey or white. This takes about 15 minutes.
WHY: Thorough cleaning prevents the broth from turning brown—you want pale, pristine tonkotsu.
Build and start the broth

Return cleaned bones to the stockpot. Add charred aromatics, leeks, scallion whites, mushrooms, and the slab of pork fatback. Cover with cold water. Bring to a rolling boil over high heat, skimming off any scum that rises (this stops after 20 minutes). Wipe the pot rim clean. Reduce to a bare simmer, cover with a heavy lid, and check after 15 minutes—adjust heat to maintain a slow rolling boil.
WHY: A rolling boil (not a gentle simmer) is what emulsifies collagen into creaminess.
Cook 12-plus hours

After 4 hours, carefully remove the pork fatback with a slotted spoon—it should be tender. Refrigerate it. Continue boiling the broth, topping up with water as needed to keep bones submerged, for 8 to 12 more hours. The broth is ready when it's opaque with the texture of light cream, about 12-14 hours total.
WHY: Long boiling breaks down collagen into gelatin and emulsifies fat, creating the signature creamy texture.
Strain and finish

Once broth is opaque and rich, strain through a fine-mesh strainer (lined with cheesecloth for extra clarity). Skim and discard any floating fat. Finely chop the cooked pork fatback and whisk it into the broth. To serve, season broth with salt, soy sauce, or a splash of sesame oil. Ladle over cooked ramen noodles and top with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, spring onions, nori, sesame seeds, and chilli oil.
WHY: Straining removes solids; whipped-in fatback adds unctuous richness; seasoning is done at the end.
pair: Clean flavours cut through richness, refresh palate

blurb: Fresh egg pasta with a slow-cooked meat sauce from Bologna.

prov.famous_for: Rich, slow-cooked meat sauce with soffritto base
prov.consists_of: Beef and pork ragù with soffritto, wine, milk/cream, tomato, served over fresh egg pasta
prov.name_origin: Ragù alla Bolognese, from Bologna, Emilia-Romagna, officially documented 1891
prov.why_loved: Deep, layered flavour, tender texture, perfect comfort food
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — slow braise with soffritto base, wine reduction, long simmer

brief.lead: Ragù Bolognese is Italy's most iconic meat sauce—a slow-cooked masterpiece from Bologna that turns humble beef and pork into something luxurious. It starts with a soffritto of finely minced onion, carrot, celery (no garlic, no herbs in the authentic version), builds with beef and pork mince, a splash of wine, a note of tomato, and a long, gentle simmer for at least three hours (some cooks go longer). The result is a rich, deeply flavoured sauce that coats fresh egg tagliatelle perfectly. This isn't tomato sauce with meat—it's meat sauce with a touch of tomato.
expect: Primarily beef and pork flavour, tomato is secondary.
Slow cooking mellows and concentrates—no harsh notes.
Thick enough to cling to fresh pasta strands.
Tagliatelle's rough surface holds the sauce.
kit: Large heavy pot or Dutch oven

ing: pancetta or unsmoked bacon, finely minced
fresh tagliatelle (or high-quality dried egg tagliatelle)
tl: Finely mince onion, carrot, celery; mince pancetta
Sauté pancetta, butter, oil, soffritto veg gently 10-15 min until soft
Add beef and pork mince, brown well, breaking up clumps
Add wine, reduce to dry; add milk/cream, reduce; add tomato purée, season
Cover and simmer very gently 2.5-3+ hours, stirring occasionally
Cook fresh tagliatelle 1-2 min in salted water, drain, toss with ragù
Serve with parmesan and a splash of olive oil
steps: Prepare the soffritto

Finely mince the onion, carrot, and celery into tiny, even pieces—this is the Italian soffritto, the flavour foundation. Also finely mince the pancetta. Consistency matters: everything should be very small so it melts into the sauce.
WHY: The soffritto provides the aromatic base—finely minced it disappears into the sauce.
Cook soffritto gently

In a large heavy pot or Dutch oven, heat olive oil and butter over medium-low heat. Add the minced pancetta and cook for 5 minutes until it releases its fat. Add the soffritto vegetables (onion, carrot, celery) and cook gently for 10-15 minutes until soft and translucent but not browned. Season lightly with salt.
WHY: Gentle cooking sweetens the vegetables without browning—this is the foundation of Bolognese flavour.
Brown the meats

Increase heat to medium. Add beef and pork mince to the pot. Break up any clumps with a spoon and cook, stirring occasionally, until the meat is well-browned all over. This should take about 10-15 minutes. Don't rush this step—browning adds deep flavour.
WHY: Well-browned meat develops Maillard reactions that provide the ragù's deep savoury character.
Add wine, milk, tomato

Pour in the white wine and cook, stirring, until completely evaporated—this concentrates the flavours. Add the milk or cream and cook again until nearly evaporated. Stir in the tomato purée and season with salt and pepper. The sauce should look thick and meaty, not tomato-heavy.
WHY: Wine adds acidity and depth; milk mellows and balances; tomato is a supporting actor, not the star.
Slow-simmer the ragù

Reduce heat to low and cover the pot. Simmer very gently for at least 2.5 to 3 hours, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. The ragù is done when the meat is tender and the sauce has thickened and darkened to a rich, deep colour. Some cooks simmer up to 4 hours.
WHY: Long, gentle simmering mellows the flavours and develops the characteristic Bolognese richness.
Cook and serve

While ragù simmers, bring a large pot of salted water to boil. Cook fresh tagliatelle for 1-2 minutes (dried egg tagliatelle may need 3-4 minutes) until al dente. Drain, reserving a splash of pasta water. Toss the pasta with the ragù, adding a splash of pasta water if needed to coat. Serve immediately with freshly grated parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil.
WHY: Fresh egg pasta is traditional—the rough surface holds the sauce; finishing with parmesan and oil is authentic Bolognese style.
pair: Italian red with acidity cuts richness, matches meat intensity

blurb: Classic Neapolitan pizza with tomato, mozzarella, and basil.

prov.famous_for: Simple toppings representing Italian flag colours on soft, puffy crust
prov.consists_of: Soft, puffy dough with San Marzano tomatoes, fior di latte mozzarella, fresh basil
prov.name_origin: Created in 1889 by Raffaele Esposito in Naples to honour Queen Margherita of Savoy
prov.why_loved: Pure expression of quality ingredients, crispy-chewy crust, bright flavours
prov.method_detail: Oven — very high temperature (450-500°C), fast bake (60-90 seconds)

brief.lead: Pizza Margherita is Naples' masterpiece and the world's most iconic pizza—soft, puffy dough with a leopard-spotted char, topped with just three ingredients in the colours of the Italian flag: red San Marzano tomatoes, white fior di latte mozzarella (or buffalo mozzarella), and green fresh basil. The dough ferments for 24-plus hours for flavour and bubbles, then bakes in screaming-hot heat (450-500°C if you can get it) for 60 to 90 seconds. The result is a crisp yet chewy crust, sweet-tart tomato, creamy cheese, aromatic basil—the perfect pizza expression of less-is-more.
expect: Soft and airy inside with leopard-spotted char on edges.
60-90 seconds at extreme heat—texture is everything.

ing: San Marzano tomatoes or peeled plum tomatoes, crushed
fior di latte mozzarella (or buffalo mozzarella), diced
tl: Mix flour, salt, yeast, water, knead until smooth (8-10 min)
Let dough rise at room temp 2 hours, then refrigerate 12-24 hours
Heat oven to max (250°C+) with pizza stone/steel for 1 hour
Divide dough into 2, shape into discs, let relax 20 min
Top with tomato, mozzarella, bake 60-90 sec in pizza oven (3-5 min home oven)
Add fresh basil, drizzle with oil, slice and eat immediately
steps: Mix the dough

Combine 500g Italian 00 flour, 10g salt, and 3g instant yeast in a large bowl. Add 325g room-temperature water and 10g olive oil (optional). Mix by hand or with a dough hook until a shaggy dough forms. Turn onto a clean surface and knead for 8-10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough should feel soft and slightly tacky, not sticky.
WHY: 00 flour has the right protein for Neapolitan texture; hydration (65%) creates the characteristic puff.
Bulk fermentation

Place dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and let rise at room temperature for 2 hours. Then refrigerate for 12 to 24 hours. This long, slow fermentation develops flavour and creates the dough's extensibility for stretching.
WHY: Long fermentation builds flavour, gluten structure, and the bubbles that char in the oven.
Preheat oven extreme

One hour before baking, place a pizza stone or baking steel in your oven and heat to its maximum setting—ideally 250°C (480°F) or higher. If you have a pizza oven that reaches 450-500°C, even better. The stone or steel must be fully preheated to create the blistered crust.
WHY: Extreme heat is the secret to the leopard-spotted char and puff—Neapolitan pizza bakes in seconds.
Shape the pizzas

Remove dough from the fridge 1-2 hours before baking to come to room temperature. Divide into 2 equal pieces (about 200g each). Shape each into a ball and let rest on a floured surface, covered, for 20 minutes. Gently stretch each ball into a 25-30cm disc using your hands—don't use a rolling pin, which would press out the gas bubbles.
WHY: Room-temperature dough stretches easily; hand-pushing preserves the bubbles that create puff.
Top minimally

Lay one stretched dough on a floured peel or inverted baking sheet. Spread with crushed San Marzano tomatoes (drained of excess liquid). Scatter diced fior di latte mozzarella over the top. Don't overload—sparse toppings ensure the crust cooks through. Have fresh basil and olive oil ready.
WHY: Minimal toppings let the dough shine—too much weight prevents proper rise.
Bake fast and serve

Slide the pizza onto the preheated stone. In a pizza oven (450-500°C), bake for 60-90 seconds. In a home oven (250°C), bake for 3-5 minutes. The crust should be puffy and charred in spots, the cheese melted and bubbling. Remove from oven, immediately scatter fresh basil leaves over the top, drizzle with olive oil, and sprinkle with flaky salt. Slice and serve immediately.
WHY: Fast baking at extreme heat creates the characteristic Neapolitan texture—crisp-chewy with leopard char. Eat while hot.
pair: Italy · frizzante red or medium red
Italian red with acidity or slight bubbles cuts richness, matches tomato

blurb: Crispy panko chicken cutlet with Japanese curry sauce over rice.

prov.famous_for: Crispy panko chicken with rich, mild curry sauce
prov.consists_of: Breaded chicken cutlet (tonkatsu), Japanese curry sauce, white rice
prov.name_origin: Katsu curry (cutlet curry), Japan, early-to-mid 20th century
prov.why_loved: Contrast of crispy chicken with velvety curry, comfort food perfection
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — breaded chicken fried until crispy, served with curry sauce

brief.lead: Katsu curry is Japan's brilliant mashup—a crispy panko-crusted chicken cutlet (tonkatsu) served over rice with a rich, mildly spiced curry sauce. The chicken gets a classic three-step breading (flour, egg, panko) and fries until golden-crisp. Meanwhile, the curry sauce builds from a roux of butter and flour, curry powder, aromatics, and stock, simmered into velvet. It's comfort food that delivers: the crunch of cutlet, the warmth of curry, the sweetness of Japanese-style curry, all over fluffy rice. A Friday night favourite.
expect: Panko breading fries light and airy—stay-crispy under sauce.
Japanese curry is sweeter, milder than Indian style.
Roux-thickened, glossy, clings to cutlet and rice.
Protein, carb, sauce in one bowl—satisfying and balanced.

ing: chicken breasts or thighs, pounded to 1.5cm thickness
fukujinzuke (pickled vegetables) or pickled red ginger
tl: Pound chicken to even thickness, season with salt and pepper
Dredge in flour, dip in egg, coat with panko
Melt butter, cook flour + curry powder 2 min, add veg, cook 3 min
Add stock, soy, ketchup, honey, simmer 10 min until thickened
Fry chicken in oil 3-4 min per side until golden and cooked through
Slice chicken into strips, serve over rice with sauce ladled over
steps: Prep the chicken

Pound chicken breasts or thighs to an even 1.5cm thickness using a meat mallet or rolling pin. Season both sides with salt and pepper. Set up three shallow bowls: one with flour, one with beaten eggs, one with panko breadcrumbs.
WHY: Even thickness cooks evenly; breading in the right order creates crisp coating that stays.
Bread the chicken

Dredge each chicken piece in flour, shaking off excess. Dip into beaten egg, letting excess drip off. Press firmly into panko breadcrumbs, coating both sides well. Place breaded chicken on a plate and let rest for 10 minutes—this helps the coating adhere.
WHY: Flour-egg-panko is the classic katsu breading; resting prevents coating from flaking.
Start the curry roux

Melt butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add flour and curry powder; cook, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until fragrant but not browned. Add diced onion, carrot, and grated apple (if using); cook for 3 minutes until softened.
WHY: The roux thickens the sauce; cooking curry powder blooms its flavours without bitterness.
Simmer the sauce

Gradually whisk in chicken stock, stirring to eliminate any lumps. Add soy sauce, ketchup, honey, and salt. Reduce heat to low and simmer for 10-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens and coats the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasoning if needed.
WHY: Simmering mellows the curry powder and develops the Japanese-style sweetness.
Fry the chicken

While sauce simmers, heat oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat. Fry breaded chicken for 3-4 minutes per side until golden-brown and crispy, and cooked through (74°C internal). Drain on paper towels.
WHY: Medium-high heat creates crisp exterior without burning; draining removes excess oil.
Slice and serve

Slice the fried chicken into strips. Serve immediately over hot white rice, ladling the curry sauce over the chicken and rice. Accompany with fukujinzuke (Japanese pickles) or pickled ginger for crunch and contrast.
WHY: Slicing makes eating easy; sauce over both chicken and rice ties the dish together; pickles add contrast.
pair: Clean flavours cut through rich curry sauce, refresh palate
Bubbly, bitter, refreshing—classic izakaya pairing with fried foods

blurb: Fried rice with egg, vegetables, and seasonings—restaurant style at home.

prov.famous_for: Simple, satisfying rice dish with fluffy texture and wok flavour
prov.consists_of: Fried rice with egg, vegetables (peas, corn, carrot), soy-based seasonings
prov.name_origin: Egg fried rice (蛋炒饭), China, traditional home-style dish
prov.why_loved: Quick, satisfying, uses leftover rice, better than takeout
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — high-heat stir-fry with proper wok technique

brief.lead: Egg fried rice is the ultimate home-cook comfort dish—takeout-style rice that's actually better made at home if you know a few techniques. Jasmine rice gets washed and cooked (fresh rice works if you fluff it well), then stir-fried over high heat with beaten eggs, mixed vegetables, and a Cantonese seasoning blend of light and dark soy sauce, salt, sugar, and chicken bouillon powder. The secret? Hot wok, constant motion, and proper seasoning. No mushy clumps, just fluffy, fragrant, slightly charred grains in under 20 minutes.
expect: Rice is separate, not clumpy—each grain coated but distinct.
Egg is cooked into rice, not scrambled separately.
Salty-savoury with depth from both soy sauces.
High-heat stir-fry takes minutes—fast weeknight dinner.

tl: Wash rice 3 times, cook with water in rice cooker, let steam 2 min
Dice carrot and green onion; beat eggs with salt
Microwave carrot, corn, peas with splash of water 2 min, drain
Fluff cooked rice with chopsticks 1-2 min to cool and separate
Heat wok, add oil, cook eggs 20-30 sec, add rice on top
Toss rice and egg 2 min, breaking clumps, press to separate
Add vegetables, toss 2 min, lower heat, add mixed seasoning
High heat, toss 2 min, add oil and green onions, toss 1 min, serve
steps: Cook and fluff rice

Wash jasmine rice three times with fresh water, draining after each rinse. Add to rice cooker with 200g water and cook. Once done, let rice steam covered for 2 additional minutes. Then fluff thoroughly with chopsticks for 1-2 minutes to release steam and separate grains. Fresh rice works when properly fluffed—day-old rice isn't mandatory.
WHY: Washing removes excess starch that causes clumping; fluffing separates grains for even frying.
Prep ingredients

While rice cooks, dice the green parts of green onions and set aside (save the whites for another dish). Dice carrot into small pieces. In a bowl, combine frozen corn and peas. Add a splash of water and microwave for 2 minutes; drain well. Crack eggs into a bowl, add 1/4 teaspoon salt, and beat lightly.
WHY: Pre-cooking vegetables ensures they're tender; microwaving is efficient; salting eggs seasons them evenly.
Heat wok and cook egg

Heat a wok or large skillet over high heat until very hot. Add oil and swirl to coat. Pour in beaten eggs and stir gently for 20-30 seconds until mostly set but still creamy. Immediately add the fluffed rice directly on top of the eggs—don't stir yet.
WHY: Cooking egg before rice prevents it from becoming scrambled bits; adding rice on top lets residual heat finish the egg gently.
Stir-fry rice and egg

Using a wok spatula, flip and stir-fry constantly for 2 minutes, breaking apart any clumps of rice and egg. Use the flat side of the spatula to press down on rice, helping separate grains. Scoop along the bottom edges of the wok, not the centre, to prevent sticking.
WHY: Constant motion prevents sticking; pressing separates grains without mashing them.
Add vegetables and season

Add the cooked vegetables and increase heat to high. Stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until everything is well mixed and hot. Meanwhile, in a small bowl, combine light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, salt, sugar, chicken bouillon powder (if using), and water. Pour this seasoning mixture over the rice.
WHY: Both soy sauces add colour and depth—light for saltiness, dark for colour; sugar balances salt.
Finish and serve

Increase heat to high and stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until the seasoning is evenly distributed and the rice is hot and fragrant. Add the remaining oil and diced green onions; toss for 1 minute. Turn off the heat. Taste and add more light soy sauce if needed (up to 1 teaspoon). Serve hot in a bowl or mounded on a plate.
WHY: Final oil adds gloss and richness; green onions add freshness at the end for bright contrast.
pair: Light, crisp beer cleanses palate between bites
Sweet-spicy bubbles refresh, complement soy flavours
